title: The Seal-In: Make the Relay Hold Itself On

questions.q: To fix the release problem, which of the relay's own contacts do you wire in parallel with the Start button?
options: One of its Normally Open contacts
One of its Normally Closed contacts
The coil itself
A spare common terminal
questions.why: A Normally Open contact is open at rest but closes when the coil energizes, so it can take over feeding the coil only after Start is pressed — exactly what a seal-in needs.

questions.q: After you release the Start button in a latched circuit, what keeps the coil energized?
options: The relay's own NO contact, now closed, feeding the coil in parallel with Start
The Start button stays mechanically stuck down
Stored energy in the coil keeps it powered
The Stopped light feeds power back to the coil
questions.why: When the coil pulled in, its seal-in contact closed and now supplies the coil all by itself, so the relay holds its own power — it is 'sealed in.'

questions.q: Why must the seal-in contact be wired in PARALLEL with the Start button, not in series with it?
options: So the contact provides a second path to the coil once Start is let go
So the contact blocks the coil until Start is pressed twice
So pressing Start opens the seal-in path
So the coil only powers when both Start and the contact are closed
questions.why: Parallel means either path can feed the coil — Start gets it going, then the closed seal-in contact carries it on its own after you release the button.

visual.caption: The seal-in circuit as drawn — at rest.

questions.q: You build the seal-in and the machine now latches on and stays running. What problem does this lesson say you still have?
options: There is no way to turn it back OFF
The coil voltage no longer matches
The Running light is now too dim
The Start button no longer works
questions.why: The latch holds itself on with nothing in the circuit to break the feed, so there is still no off switch — the Stop button comes next.

questions.q: An operator says a conveyor motor 'runs fine while I hold Start but drops out the second I let go' — every single time, no exceptions. What's the most likely cause?
options: The coil's suppression diode is installed backwards
The main power contacts are pitted and dropping voltage under motor load
The seal-in contact is open-circuit or its wiring is broken, so nothing feeds the coil once Start releases
The Stop button has a sneak path to neutral
questions.why: A seal-in that only runs while the button is held is the classic signature of an open or disconnected seal-in contact — there's no second path to carry the coil after Start opens. A sneak path (last option) causes the opposite symptom: the machine won't stop. A backwards diode or pitted power contacts don't produce a clean 'only while held' pattern.

visual.caption: The print — trace every path that can feed CR1 after Start is released.

questions.q: You put a meter on the coil terminals while pressing Start on a 24VDC seal-in circuit. The reading dips to 17V for about 30ms before settling at 24V, and the relay pulls in fine with no chatter. What do you conclude?
options: 17V is below the 85% pull-in threshold, so the relay should be replaced
This proves a sneak path is feeding the coil from the Stop side
The seal-in contact is pitted and must be cleaned immediately
This is normal inrush sag — the coil pulled in cleanly, so no action is needed
questions.why: A brief inrush sag that recovers and results in a clean, non-chattering pull-in is exactly what the module describes as normal — inrush current is 6-10x sealed current for 15-50ms while the armature closes. The concern threshold (below ~20V, or under 85% of rated) only matters if it prevents pull-in or causes chatter, which didn't happen here.

questions.q: You're wiring a seal-in circuit and need to pick the coil supply's current rating. The relay's coil datasheet lists 80mA sealed current. What should you size the supply to handle, and why?
options: Exactly 80mA, matching the sealed current rating on the datasheet
Well above the inrush current (roughly 480-800mA for this coil), because sizing for sealed current alone lets the supply sag and the relay chatter
The auxiliary contact rating of 10A, since that's the highest number on the relay
Half the sealed current, since inrush only lasts milliseconds and doesn't count
questions.why: The module is explicit: always size the supply for inrush (6-10x sealed, here roughly 480-800mA), not sealed current, or the rail sags during pull-in and the coil chatters at inrush current, pitting the seal-in contact. Matching only the 80mA sealed rating (first option) is the mistake that causes the sag in the first place.

questions.q: A machine has been latching fine every morning for weeks but starts randomly dropping out mid-shift on hot afternoons and after rain. Where do you put your meter, and what reading confirms a bad seal-in contact rather than a coil or supply problem?
options: At the 24VDC power supply terminals with no load; any reading below 24V confirms the contact is bad
Across the coil during the initial Start press only; a brief dip below 24V confirms the contact is bad
At the Stop button terminals with the circuit de-energized; continuity confirms the contact is bad
Across the closed seal-in contact itself; a reading of roughly 2-8V while latched confirms a pitted/oxidized high-resistance contact
questions.why: The module's intermittent-latch failure mode calls for measuring voltage across the closed seal-in contact itself (not the supply, not the coil at Start press) — a good contact reads under 0.5V, while 2-8V across it while 'closed' means it's pitted or oxidized and dropping voltage the coil needs. Checking the unloaded supply (first option) won't reveal a downstream contact problem, and normal Start-press inrush dip (second option) is unrelated to contact pitting.

questions.q: You skip the suppression diode on a 24VDC seal-in coil to save a part and finish the panel faster. What is the real consequence over time?
options: The coil will simply fail to pull in on the next Start press
The seal-in contact will weld permanently shut the first time it closes
Every time the coil drops out, the inductive kickback can spike to 200-400V across the seal-in contact, arc-pitting it on every single cycle
Nothing changes functionally, it only affects the Running indicator light
questions.why: Per the module's field notes, without a suppression diode the inductive kickback at dropout can reach 200-400V on a 24VDC circuit, and that spike arcs across the seal-in contact every time it opens — accelerating pitting and shortening contact life dramatically. It doesn't prevent pull-in (first option) or weld the contact shut on first closure (second option); the damage is cumulative arc erosion at dropout, not an instant failure.

questions.q: You're commissioning a new motor starter and have a choice of two spare auxiliary contacts on the starter to use as the seal-in: one rated 10A/120VAC, and one you find on an old spare relay pulled from a drawer rated only for signal-level duty (dry contact, low milliamp). Which do you use for the seal-in, and why?
options: The 10A/120VAC auxiliary contact, because the seal-in must be rated for the coil's inrush current, not just its trivial sealed current
The signal-level contact, because the seal-in only ever carries the small sealed coil current so a low-rated contact is fine
Either one — seal-in contacts don't carry real current, they just signal that the coil is on
Neither — the seal-in must always be a brand-new contact, reused contacts are never acceptable
questions.why: The seal-in contact has to survive the coil's inrush every time it makes or breaks, not just the trivial 50-100mA sealed current — that's why real auxiliary contacts on starters/relays carry ratings like 10A at 120VAC. A signal-level dry contact isn't built for that repeated inrush arc and will pit or fail quickly. It's the auxiliary contact rating that matters, not just proximity to a coil that draws little current at steady state.
